title: Improving Contractor Cybersecurity Act
plain_summary: This bill says that technology companies must have special programs to let people report computer security problems before they can work with the government. When someone finds a security bug, the company has to tell a government cybersecurity agency about it within seven days. The company also has to promise not to sue people who report problems in good faith. The government agency then puts information about these security problems into special databases so other people can protect themselves too.
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Oversight and Government Reform.

sections.title: Section 1 Short Title
sections.text: This law can be called the Improving Contractor Cybersecurity Act.

sections.title: Section 2 Vulnerability Disclosure Policy and Program Required for Information Technology Contractors
sections.text: This section adds new rules to federal law about computer security. Government agencies cannot hire technology companies unless those companies have proper security reporting systems. These companies must have a written policy that explains how people can report computer security problems they find. The policy must describe which computer systems people can test for problems. It must explain what kind of testing is allowed and what is not allowed. If the systems contain sensitive information, the company must set rules about how people can access that information when looking for security problems. The policy must tell people exactly how to report security problems, including where to send reports like a website form or email address. It must explain what information is needed to describe the problem clearly. The policy must promise that anyone can report problems without giving their name. Companies must promise they will not sue people who accidentally break rules while trying to help find security problems in good faith. If someone else sues a security researcher who was following the company rules, the company must tell the court that the person was following their policy. The policy must explain how long it takes to respond to reports and what steps the company will take to fix problems. It must give clear guidelines about what researchers can and cannot do when looking for security problems. The policy cannot require researchers to give personal information about themselves. It cannot limit testing to only people the company approves but must let anyone from the public look for and report security problems. Companies must also describe how they will communicate with researchers and when communication happens. They must explain their target timelines for responding to reports, checking if problems are real, and fixing problems. Companies must have a webpage where anyone can report security problems with their technology. The page must list contact information for the team that reviews reports. It must describe how long reviews take and whether the company pays money to people who find security problems. If a company finds a security problem they cannot fix themselves, they must report it to whoever can fix it or tell the researcher who to contact. Companies must report certain security problems to the Cybersecurity and Infrastructure Security Agency within seven days of publishing their policy and whenever they receive new reports. They must report any real security problem that was not known to the public before, especially if it affects software or services used by other government agencies or businesses. The Cybersecurity and Infrastructure Security Agency must share these security problems with national databases that track computer vulnerabilities. This section defines executive agency as having the same meaning as defined elsewhere in federal law. A researcher means the person who reports a security problem. Information technology has the same meaning as defined in another part of federal law. These new rules take effect when this law is signed and apply to any contract signed after that date.
